facade_colour: dark brown/dark brick upper; white/cream tile or painted brick at ground level; brick (upper), mixed at ground (painted brick/tile)

hcd_data.architectural_style: Commercial (38 Kensington Avenue), Converted House-form (40 Kensington Avenue), Semi-detached, Bay-andGable (40 Kensington Avenue) (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property at 38 Kensington Avenue has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two- storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure. The property at 40 Kensington Avenue has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the addition at 38 Kensington Avenue has retained building features including its brick facade with pressed metal cornice and second storey window openings with brick and stone voussoirs. The property at 40 Kensington Avenue retains original building features including its cross-gabled roofline, front gable with decorative wooden brackets, and projecting bay and fenestration pattern above the first storey. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area. With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, each property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, each property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

facade_description: 38 Kensington Ave is a commercial building with a street-level storefront. The street frontage spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys beneath a gable, consistent with its commercial, converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The frontage is defined primarily by brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Key storefront and facade features include bay window, decorative brick, cornice, gable brackets.
heritage_summary: The property at 40 Kensington Avenue retains original building features including its cross-gabled roofline, front gable with decorative wooden brackets, and projecting bay and fenestration pattern above the first storey. With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, each property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

windows_detail.note: Large storefront windows flanking entrance. Left window has yellow sign 'BUY SELL TRADE'. Right window displays merchandise. Glazed storefront with white tile/brick bulkhead below.
Brown/dark-framed windows on brown-painted or dark brick facade.
Small window in gable with white louvered vent or shutter.

doors_observed.note: Central recessed entrance with 'STILL TRADING' and 'LOST BOYS' signage

storefront_observed.signage_text: LOST BOYS (large graffiti-style sign on right pilaster, orange/white on dark), STILL TRADING (yellow text above door), BUY SELL TRADE (yellow banner in left window)
storefront_observed.note: Distinctive storefront with diamond-pattern security grille above the shopfront. White subway-tile or glazed brick bulkhead below windows. Track lighting illuminates interior. Orange 'Lost Boys' graffiti-style logo on right column. Yellow signage. Decorative diamond lattice metal screen above storefront acts as fascia.

diamond_lattice_screen.note: Decorative diamond-pattern metal lattice screen spanning full width above storefront
